findings.summary: PRECISION class constants on all 8 case handlers are never wired into the model's precision system and have no runtime effect
evidence: Each XxxCase class defines a PRECISION constant (e.g. NominativeCase.PRECISION = 1.5, VocativeCase.PRECISION = 2.0)
_apply_case_transformation in active_inference.py calls self.get_precision(self._case), which reads from self._precision_weights
self._precision_weights is initialized to {case: 1.0 for case in Case} in Model.__init__ and is never populated from the case handler PRECISION constants
No caller in the codebase connects XxxCase.PRECISION to model.set_precision(); the constants are dead values
findings.suggestion: Either remove the PRECISION constants if they are purely aspirational documentation, or wire them in: in each XxxCase.apply() call model.set_precision(Case.X, XxxCase.PRECISION) before returning, so _apply_case_transformation actually scales from the intended value rather than always 1.0.

findings.summary: Two parallel Model base classes (src/core/model.py and src/models/base.py) expose divergent contracts with no clear sunset for the shim
evidence: src/models/base.py defines a Model class with different __init__ signature (name: str, case: Case) vs src/core/model.py (name: str = None, parameters: Dict)
src/models/base.py Model has no _apply_case_transformation hook, no _case_history, no connections list, and raises TypeError on non-Case assignment — differing invariants from core Model
src/models/base.py has 22 importers; no comment or deprecation warning indicates it is a transitional shim with a removal plan
Code that imports from src.models.base gets a Model that silently ignores case transformations (no _apply_case_transformation body)
findings.suggestion: Add a deprecation warning in src/models/base.py Model.__init__ pointing to src.core.model.Model, document a migration timeline, and convert the highest-traffic importers in a single sweep so the shim's 22 importers shrink toward zero.

findings.summary: src/transformations/case_transformations.py:transform_case is a one-liner passthrough that adds no policy — it only sets model.case
evidence: transform_case(model, target_case) stores original_case but never uses it, then calls model.case = target_case and returns model
revert_case() is equally thin: it just calls model.case = model._prior_case
All callers could write model.case = target_case directly; the wrapper adds a layer but zero policy, validation, or translation
findings.suggestion: Either delete the module and update callers to use the case property directly, or give it a genuine reason to exist: add validation (is target_case a valid Case member?), emit a domain event, or enforce transition guards — something that justifies the indirection.

findings.summary: Visualization modules use absolute src.X imports while their sibling animal_visualization.py uses relative imports — inconsistent within the same package
evidence: src/visualization/case_visualization.py: from src.core.model import Model, Case
src/visualization/case_comparison.py: from src.core.model import Model, Case
src/visualization/animal_visualization.py: from ..core.model import Case (relative)
src/transformations/case_transformations.py uses absolute from src.core.model while src/cases/*.py use relative from ..core.model
findings.suggestion: Pick one convention for the package — relative imports are standard for intra-package references in Python. Convert the absolute src.X imports in visualization/ and transformations/ to relative form to match the style used everywhere in src/cases/ and src/models/.

findings.summary: Numerous inline comments restate exactly what the adjacent code does without adding insight
evidence: active_inference.py line 38: '# Initialize model dimensions based on inputs or defaults' before three lines that do exactly that
active_inference.py line 65: '# Posterior beliefs (these will be updated during inference)' above posterior_means = self.prior_means.copy()
active_inference.py line 86: '# Ensure required parameters are available (if parameters dict was provided)' above 'if parameters is not None:'
insect/base.py line 81: '# Initialize with default parameters for active inference' above the literal dict
nominative.py line 30-32: comment block explaining that model.case = Case.NOMINATIVE 'will trigger _apply_case_transformation internally'
findings.suggestion: Remove comments that echo the code. Retain only comments that explain WHY a decision was made (e.g. the base-precision comment in active_inference.py line 69-72 is a good example of a useful why-comment).

findings.summary: _select_action_by_case builds a full dispatch dict on every call and falls back to _select_default_action which is unreachable given all 8 cases are covered
evidence: _select_action_by_case (line 449-471) builds a dict keyed on all 8 Case enum values on every invocation
The dict covers all 8 Case members; dict.get() fallback to _select_default_action can never be reached because Case has exactly 8 values all present in the dict
_select_default_action exists solely as this dead fallback
Building the full dict on every call is wasteful; a class-level mapping or direct getattr dispatch would be cleaner
findings.suggestion: Promote the dispatch table to a class-level attribute (built once) and remove the dead _select_default_action. If future-proofing for unknown cases is genuinely needed, add an explicit raise NotImplementedError rather than a silent idle fallback.

findings.summary: Case update methods mix return-dict-with-error-status and direct dict literals inconsistently, with no predictable contract for callers
evidence: _update_accusative returns {"status": "error", "message": ...} when data is None
_update_dative returns {"status": "error", "message": ...} when data is None
_update_instrumental returns {"status": "error", ...} for unknown method
_update_ablative returns {"status": "error", ...} for unrecognized data type
_update_nominative never returns an error status — it silently succeeds even on invalid input
Callers have no documented contract; some methods raise nothing, others return status dicts. No caller in tests checks status=='error'
findings.suggestion: Establish a single error strategy: either raise ValueError/TypeError at the boundary (simpler, lets callers use normal exception handling) or return a typed Result/NamedTuple consistently. The current mix of silent success and dict-based errors makes callers unpredictable.

findings.summary: Tests confirm PRECISION constants exist but no test verifies they have any effect on model behavior during case transitions
evidence: test_case_handlers.py line 41: test_precision_constant asserts AccusativeCase.PRECISION == 1.2 — tests the constant value only
No test verifies that after AccusativeCase.apply(model), model.get_precision(Case.ACCUSATIVE) == AccusativeCase.PRECISION
No test verifies that after a case transition, likelihood_precision or prior_precision is scaled by the case-specific PRECISION value
Given the wiring gap identified above, these tests pass but provide false confidence
findings.suggestion: Add integration tests that apply a case handler to an ActiveInferenceModel and assert the resulting precision matrices reflect the intended case-specific scaling, not just that the constant attribute exists.

findings.summary: All 8 XxxCase classes are collections of @staticmethod with no shared protocol/ABC, making the dispatch in CaseManager type-unsafe
evidence: CaseManager.case_handlers maps Case enums to handler classes and calls handler.apply(model)
No ABC or Protocol enforces that every handler has apply(), process_update(), calculate_free_energy()
NominativeCase and AccusativeCase both have get_parameters(); AblativeCase and VocativeCase have different extra methods (emit_to, invoke, broadcast) — no shared interface
Adding a 9th case requires knowing by convention which methods to implement; mypy cannot catch missing methods
findings.suggestion: Define a CaseHandler Protocol (or ABC) with the required static methods: apply, process_update, calculate_free_energy. Register it and have each class satisfy it so type checkers enforce the contract.

findings.summary: InsectModel domain defines extra cases (PheromonalCase, StigmergicCase, CasteCase, SwarmCase, SubstrateCase, MetamorphicCase) outside the core Case enum, creating a split case system with no common interface
evidence: src/models/insect/cases/ contains at least 6 domain-specific case modules that are entirely separate from the 8-value Case enum in src/core/model.py
InsectModel.transform_case accepts only core Case values; the domain cases are applied via separate utility classes, not via the same setter/transformation pipeline
A reader of src/core/model.py has no visibility into the insect extension cases; the architecture contract (8 cases) is violated in practice
findings.suggestion: Document the extension mechanism explicitly: either add an ExtendedCase registry concept to the framework or document in CLAUDE.md and the InsectModel docstring that domain cases extend the system via a separate protocol. Currently the existence of 6 extra cases is a silent architectural surprise.
